source_text: I read some books last month.

reference_translations.text: Ho letto qualche libro il mese scorso.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Qualche + singular noun; the plural meaning lives in qualche.

reference_translations.text: Ho letto alcuni libri il mese scorso.
reference_translations.notes: Equally standard: alcuni takes the plural noun instead. Both are fully correct.

reference_translations.text: Ho letto qualche libri il mese scorso.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: qualche always takes the singular. The near-universal calque from English, and the single most important error in this topic.

explanation: Two correct routes: Ho letto qualche libro or Ho letto alcuni libri. Qualche is invariable and forces the singular; alcuni agrees and takes the plural. Both mean 'some books'. What Italian will not accept is the English shape with both plurals at once.

source_text: A few students arrived late this morning.

reference_translations.text: Qualche studente è arrivato in ritardo stamattina.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Note the singular verb: qualche + singular noun is singular throughout.

reference_translations.text: Alcuni studenti sono arrivati in ritardo stamattina.
reference_translations.notes: The alcuni route, with everything plural. Equally correct.

reference_translations.text: Qualche studenti sono arrivati in ritardo stamattina.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG twice over: qualche takes the singular noun, and that singular noun then takes a singular verb.

explanation: A few students arrived late this morning. The qualche route keeps everything singular (qualche studente è arrivato); the alcuni route makes everything plural (alcuni studenti sono arrivati). What you cannot do is mix them: the agreement has to follow the quantifier you picked all the way to the verb.

source_text: Some girls arrived early and helped us.

reference_translations.text: Alcune ragazze sono arrivate presto e ci hanno aiutato.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Alcune agrees with the feminine plural ragazze.

reference_translations.text: Delle ragazze sono arrivate presto e ci hanno aiutato.
reference_translations.notes: The partitive alternative, natural and correct.

reference_translations.text: Alcuni ragazze sono arrivate presto e ci hanno aiutato.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: alcuni is masculine; ragazze is feminine, so alcune. This is the bucket's named common miss.

explanation: Some girls arrived early and helped us: Alcune ragazze sono arrivate presto e ci hanno aiutato. Unlike qualche, alcuni agrees with its noun, so a feminine plural gives alcune. Delle ragazze is the partitive and says much the same thing.
examiner_note: The delle reference carries an explicit credit: 0.9 per §3, because a prose note alone under-specifies the number for the AI marker (Rev 17-iv, and Rev 20-i checked: delle is not this bucket's named common_miss, which is 'alcuni with a feminine noun').

source_text: I go to the gym every day before work.

reference_translations.text: Vado in palestra ogni giorno prima di lavorare.
reference_translations.notes: Standard: ogni + bare singular.

reference_translations.text: Vado in palestra tutti i giorni prima di lavorare.
reference_translations.notes: The tutti route: plural noun AND the article. Equally standard and equally common.

reference_translations.text: Vado in palestra ogni giorni prima di lavorare.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: ogni is invariable and takes a singular noun.

reference_translations.text: Vado in palestra tutti giorni prima di lavorare.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: tutti keeps the article. Tutti I giorni.

explanation: Two ways to say every day, and they are built quite differently. Ogni giorno takes a bare singular noun and no article; tutti i giorni takes a plural noun and keeps the article. Both are everyday Italian. The two errors are mirror images: giving ogni a plural, or taking tutti's article away.

source_text: Yesterday I worked all day without stopping.

reference_translations.text: Ieri ho lavorato tutto il giorno senza fermarmi.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. The article after tutto is compulsory.

reference_translations.text: Ieri ho lavorato tutta la giornata senza fermarmi.
reference_translations.notes: Equally standard with giornata; the pattern is identical.

reference_translations.text: Ieri ho lavorato tutto giorno senza fermarmi.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the article cannot be dropped. Tutto IL giorno.

explanation: Yesterday I worked all day without stopping: Ieri ho lavorato tutto il giorno senza fermarmi. English gives you nothing to copy the article from, which is exactly why it goes missing. Tutto sits in front of the article-plus-noun block; it does not replace the article.

source_text: The whole class came to the party on Saturday.

reference_translations.text: Tutta la classe è venuta alla festa sabato.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Tutta agrees with classe (f. sg.) and the article stays.

reference_translations.text: Tutto la classe è venuta alla festa sabato.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: tutto agrees with the noun, and classe is feminine singular.

reference_translations.text: Tutta classe è venuta alla festa sabato.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the article stays. Tutta LA classe.

explanation: The whole class came to the party on Saturday: Tutta la classe è venuta alla festa sabato. Two jobs land in the same two words: tutto agrees like any adjective (classe is feminine singular, so tutta), and the article survives underneath it.

source_text: Is there something interesting to see in this city?

reference_translations.text: C'è qualcosa di interessante da vedere in questa città?
reference_translations.notes: Standard. DI before the adjective, DA before the infinitive: both joints in one sentence.

reference_translations.text: C'è qualcosa interessante da vedere in questa città?
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: an adjective after qualcosa needs di.

reference_translations.text: C'è qualcosa di interessante di vedere in questa città?
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: an infinitive after qualcosa needs da, not di.

explanation: Is there something interesting to see in this city? C'è qualcosa di interessante da vedere in questa città? This sentence needs both little words at once, and they are not interchangeable: di introduces the adjective, da introduces the verb. Getting the pair right in one sentence is the real test.

source_text: I'm hungry. I'd like something good to eat.

reference_translations.text: Ho fame. Vorrei qualcosa di buono da mangiare.
reference_translations.notes: Standard: di + adjective, da + infinitive.

reference_translations.text: Ho fame. Vorrei qualcosa buono da mangiare.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the adjective needs di.

reference_translations.text: Ho fame. Vorrei qualcosa di buona da mangiare.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the adjective after qualcosa is always masculine singular.

explanation: I'm hungry, I'd like something good to eat: Ho fame. Vorrei qualcosa di buono da mangiare. Note buono and not buona: the adjective after qualcosa is fixed masculine singular, because qualcosa has no gender of its own to pass on.

source_text: I have no friends here in this city.

reference_translations.text: Non ho nessun amico qui in questa città.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Amico starts with a vowel, so nessun, following un amico. Note the singular: nessuno is never plural.

reference_translations.text: Non ho amici qui in questa città.
reference_translations.notes: Natural and correct, but it sidesteps nessuno altogether.

reference_translations.text: Non ho nessuni amici qui in questa città.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: nessuno has no plural form.

reference_translations.text: Ho nessun amico qui in questa città.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the non is compulsory. Italian wants both halves of the negative.

explanation: I have no friends here in this city: Non ho nessun amico qui in questa città. Two things English speakers trip on. Nessuno never goes plural, however plural 'friends' is; and the non cannot be dropped, because Italian wants both halves of the negative at once. The word order rule is the negation topic's; the form of nessun is this one's.
examiner_note: The bare-plural reference carries credit: 0.9 explicitly (§3: prose notes under-specify the number). 'Non ho amici' is correct Italian that avoids the drilled word rather than misusing it -- the Rev 17(iv) dodge, and not this bucket's named common_miss.

source_text: I didn't say anything to anybody about it.

reference_translations.text: Non ho detto niente a nessuno.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Three negatives in one clause and entirely ordinary.

reference_translations.text: Non ho detto nulla a nessuno.
reference_translations.notes: Nulla for niente: interchangeable, a shade more formal.

reference_translations.text: Ho detto niente a nessuno.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the non is compulsory.

reference_translations.text: Non ho detto qualcosa a qualcuno.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: qualcosa and qualcuno are the positive twins; a negative sentence needs niente and nessuno.

explanation: I didn't say anything to anybody: Non ho detto niente a nessuno. English allows only one negative per clause and switches to 'anything/anybody' to avoid a second; Italian does the opposite and piles them up. Three negatives here, and it is the normal way to say it. Niente and nulla are interchangeable.

source_text: Not one student passed the test.

reference_translations.text: Nessuno studente ha superato la prova.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Studente begins with s + consonant, so the long form: nessuno studente, like uno studente.

reference_translations.text: Nessuno studente ha passato la prova.
reference_translations.notes: Passare for superare: acceptable and common.

reference_translations.text: Nessun studente ha superato la prova.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: s + consonant forces the long form. Uno studente, nessuno studente.

reference_translations.text: Nessuno studente hanno superato la prova.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: nessuno + singular noun takes a singular verb.

explanation: Not one student passed the test: Nessuno studente ha superato la prova. Studente opens with s followed by a consonant, and that is one of the sound patterns forcing the long article (uno studente). Nessuno copies the article exactly, so the -o stays. Note also that no non appears: when nessuno comes BEFORE the verb, the non drops.

source_text: I have no idea where he's gone. [elide the indefinite before the vowel]

reference_translations.text: Non ho nessun'idea di dove sia andato.
reference_translations.notes: Standard, with the elision: idea is feminine and vowel-initial, so nessuna elides to nessun' exactly as una elides to un'idea. The apostrophe is not optional in careful writing.

reference_translations.text: Non ho nessuna idea di dove sia andato.
reference_translations.notes: Attested and widely accepted; the unelided feminine survives here in a way it does not with the article.

reference_translations.text: Non ne ho idea.
reference_translations.notes: The idiomatic everyday phrase, but it avoids nessuno entirely.

reference_translations.text: Non ho nessun idea di dove sia andato.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG as written: without the apostrophe, nessun is the MASCULINE form, and idea is feminine. The apostrophe is what marks the elided feminine.

explanation: I have no idea where he's gone: Non ho nessun'idea di dove sia andato. Idea is feminine and starts with a vowel, which is where Italian elides: un'idea, and so nessun'idea. The apostrophe is doing real work, not decoration: without it, nessun is simply the masculine form and no longer agrees with idea.
examiner_note: THIS ITEM EXISTS BECAUSE THE GRAMMAR ENGINE CANNOT SEE THE APOSTROPHE. norm() folds an apostrophe to a space and collapses whitespace (housing/js/norm.js:106, verified not assumed), so norm("nessun'idea") and norm("nessun idea") are the identical string "nessun idea": no substring markpoint can ever separate the correct elision from the missing one. The AI marker reads the raw glyph, so the diagnostic is reachable here and only here. This is the article tree's ratified position (apostrophe taught in explanations, graded in translation) applied to nessuno. Paired with ind_nn_05, which deliberately does NOT pretend to test it. The bracketed instruction is stripped by the marker per Rev 20 and must not be rendered in Italian.

source_text: Each of us has a different task to do.

reference_translations.text: Ognuno di noi ha un compito diverso da fare.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Ognuno is the subject and is singular, so ha.

reference_translations.text: Ciascuno di noi ha un compito diverso da fare.
reference_translations.notes: Ciascuno for ognuno: correct, a shade more formal.

reference_translations.text: Ognuno di noi hanno un compito diverso da fare.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: ognuno is the subject, not noi, so the verb is singular.

reference_translations.text: Ognuno di noi abbiamo un compito diverso da fare.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: agreeing with noi instead of with the head, ognuno.

explanation: Each of us has a different task to do: Ognuno di noi ha un compito diverso da fare. The plural di noi sits right beside the verb and pulls hard, but it is not the subject: ognuno is, and ognuno is singular. Italian agrees with the head of the phrase, so ha.

source_text: Whatever he does, he does it with great commitment.

reference_translations.text: Qualunque cosa faccia, la fa con grande impegno.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Qualunque cosa + subjunctive.

reference_translations.text: Qualsiasi cosa faccia, la fa con grande impegno.
reference_translations.notes: Qualsiasi for qualunque: interchangeable.

reference_translations.text: Qualunque cosa fa, la fa con grande impegno.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG in careful Italian: the free-choice words take the subjunctive in a subordinate clause, so faccia.

reference_translations.text: Qualunqui cosa faccia, la fa con grande impegno.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: qualunque never inflects.

explanation: Whatever he does, he does it with great commitment: Qualunque cosa faccia, la fa con grande impegno. English 'whatever' is one word; Italian needs two, because qualunque is an adjective and cosa gives it the noun it requires. And in a subordinate clause like this one, the free-choice words pull the subjunctive: faccia, not fa.
examiner_note: The subjunctive is graded here rather than in grammar, per the two-sided contract: the grammar items supply the mood, and the mood-choice leaf this would cite does not yet exist. See inter_chat/Architecture_IndefiniteAuthor_free_choice_mood_seam.md. The optional_bucket cites the PARENT deliberately -- the free-choice trigger has no leaf to point at, which is the whole finding.

source_text: Any person holding a ticket can come in.

reference_translations.text: Qualsiasi persona in possesso di un biglietto può entrare.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Qualsiasi is an adjective, so it can lead persona.

reference_translations.text: Qualunque persona in possesso di un biglietto può entrare.
reference_translations.notes: Qualunque for qualsiasi: interchangeable.

reference_translations.text: Chiunque abbia un biglietto può entrare.
reference_translations.notes: Correct and more natural: chiunque REPLACES the whole noun phrase rather than modifying it, and pulls the subjunctive with it.

reference_translations.text: Chiunque persona in possesso di un biglietto può entrare.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: chiunque is a pronoun and can never stand in front of a noun. This is the error the item exists to catch.

explanation: Any person holding a ticket can come in: Qualsiasi persona in possesso di un biglietto può entrare. Chiunque means nearly the same thing but is a pronoun only: it can swallow the whole phrase (Chiunque abbia un biglietto) but can never sit in front of persona. Adjective or pronoun is the whole distinction between the two words.
examiner_note: THE ADDITIVE ERROR, ROUTED HERE BY CRITERION 18. 'Chiunque persona' contains the correct answer 'chiunque' at clean word boundaries, so on any short item with an open subject slot the positive fires FIRST (any_phrases is tried before must_not_include) and credits the very error being tested. No anchoring reaches it: the wrong answer ADDS a word rather than extending one. §18's instruction is explicit -- route to MCQ or the AI strand, never leave a guard that implies a catch. It is caught two ways: ind_chi_02 by index-scoring, and here by the AI marker with an explicit negative anchor.

source_text: Ho comprato qualche libro in libreria.

reference_translations.text: I bought some books at the bookshop.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Qualche libro is PLURAL in meaning despite the singular noun.

reference_translations.text: I bought a few books at the bookshop.
reference_translations.notes: 'A few' for qualche: equally good.

reference_translations.text: I bought a book at the bookshop.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: qualche libro means several. Reading the singular noun as a singular meaning is the comprehension error this item tests.

explanation: Ho comprato qualche libro in libreria means 'I bought some books at the bookshop.' Going into Italian the trap is writing qualche libri; coming out of it, the trap is the mirror image: reading qualche libro as one book. The noun is singular but the meaning is not, because the plural lives in qualche.

source_text: Alcuni dicono che il film è bello, altri no.

reference_translations.text: Some say the film is good, others don't.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Alcuni stands alone as a pronoun: 'some people'.

reference_translations.text: Some people say the film is good, others don't.
reference_translations.notes: Spelling out 'people': natural and correct.

explanation: Alcuni dicono che il film è bello, altri no means 'Some say the film is good, others don't.' Here alcuni has no noun to lean on and stands as a pronoun in its own right, meaning some people. The pairing with altri is what signals it. Qualche could never do this: it always needs a noun.

source_text: Ogni volta che lo vedo, mi saluta calorosamente.

reference_translations.text: Every time I see him, he greets me warmly.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: Each time I see him, he says hello warmly.
reference_translations.notes: 'Each time' for ogni volta: fine.

explanation: Ogni volta che lo vedo, mi saluta calorosamente means 'Every time I see him, he greets me warmly.' Ogni volta is fixed and singular, and English matches it here with a singular 'every time', which makes this the easy direction. The hard direction is the other one.

source_text: Ho aspettato tutta la mattina, ma non è venuto nessuno.

reference_translations.text: I waited all morning, but nobody came.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: I waited the whole morning, but no one turned up.
reference_translations.notes: 'The whole morning' for tutta la mattina: fine.

explanation: Ho aspettato tutta la mattina, ma non è venuto nessuno means 'I waited all morning, but nobody came.' Two indefinites in one sentence. Tutta la mattina keeps its article, which English drops; and non è venuto nessuno uses two negatives where English allows only one, which is why it comes out as a single 'nobody'.

source_text: Qualcuno ha lasciato un messaggio per te.

reference_translations.text: Somebody left a message for you.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: Someone has left a message for you.
reference_translations.notes: Perfect tense in English: fine here.

explanation: Qualcuno ha lasciato un messaggio per te means 'Somebody left a message for you.' Qualcuno is for people and is grammatically singular, which the verb ha confirms. English 'somebody' works exactly the same way, so this direction is straightforward.

source_text: Non c'è nessuno in casa a quest'ora.

reference_translations.text: There's nobody at home at this hour.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Two Italian negatives collapse to one English one.

reference_translations.text: There isn't anyone at home at this time.
reference_translations.notes: The 'not... anyone' route: equally correct English.

reference_translations.text: There isn't nobody at home at this hour.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG in standard English: the double negative does not survive the crossing. This is the reverse calque.

explanation: Non c'è nessuno in casa a quest'ora means 'There's nobody at home at this hour.' Italian requires both non and nessuno; English forbids the pair and makes you choose: either 'there's nobody' or 'there isn't anyone', never both. The reverse calque is the interesting error in this direction.

source_text: Nessun problema, ci penso io.

reference_translations.text: No problem, I'll take care of it.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Nessun problema is the everyday 'no worries'.

reference_translations.text: No problem, I'll deal with it.
reference_translations.notes: Fine.

explanation: Nessun problema, ci penso io means 'No problem, I'll take care of it.' Worth having by heart: it is the ordinary Italian for 'no worries'. Note ci penso io, where the io at the end is doing emphasis rather than grammar: 'I'll be the one to see to it.'

source_text: Ciascun candidato deve presentare la domanda entro venerdì.

reference_translations.text: Each candidate must submit the application by Friday.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. The register is formal, and English 'each' carries it.

reference_translations.text: Every candidate must hand in their application by Friday.
reference_translations.notes: 'Every' for ciascuno: acceptable, though it loses a little of the one-by-one sense.

explanation: Ciascun candidato deve presentare la domanda entro venerdì means 'Each candidate must submit the application by Friday.' Ciascuno leans formal and belongs in a notice like this one; ognuno would sound conversational in the same sentence. English 'each' rather than 'every' keeps the one-at-a-time sense.

source_text: Non è un vino speciale, è un vino qualunque.

reference_translations.text: It isn't a special wine, it's just an ordinary wine.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Post-nominal qualunque is dismissive: run-of-the-mill.

reference_translations.text: It's not a special wine, it's a perfectly ordinary one.
reference_translations.notes: Fine; 'perfectly ordinary' catches the shrug.

reference_translations.text: It isn't a special wine, it's any wine.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: after the noun, qualunque stops meaning free choice and turns dismissive. 'Any wine' is the pre-nominal reading imported into the wrong slot.

explanation: Non è un vino speciale, è un vino qualunque means 'It isn't a special wine, it's just an ordinary wine.' Position changes the meaning. Before a noun, qualunque is free choice: qualunque vino, any wine you like. After it, the word turns dismissive: un vino qualunque, a wine like any other. Same word, opposite flavour.

source_text: Chiunque sia stato, ha fatto un ottimo lavoro.

reference_translations.text: Whoever it was, they did an excellent job.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Singular 'they' is the natural English for an unknown person.

reference_translations.text: Whoever did it, they did an excellent job.
reference_translations.notes: Fine.

reference_translations.text: Anyone was, did an excellent job.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: chiunque here is 'whoever', opening a clause, not a bare 'anyone'.

explanation: Chiunque sia stato, ha fatto un ottimo lavoro means 'Whoever it was, they did an excellent job.' Chiunque covers both English words: 'anyone' when it is the plain subject (chiunque può farlo) and 'whoever' when it opens a clause like this one. The subjunctive sia stato is chiunque's doing, and English marks none of it.
